name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٨)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for Tomorrow, and be wary of Allah. Allah is indeed well aware of what you do (18)
```

```arabic
وَلَا تَكُونُوا۟ كَٱلَّذِينَ نَسُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَأَنسَىٰهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
Do not be like those who forget Allah, so He makes them forget their own souls. It is they who are the transgressors. (19)
```

```arabic
لَا يَسْتَوِىٓ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلنَّارِ وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَنَّةِ هُمُ ٱلْفَآئِزُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
Not equal are the inmates of the Fire and the inhabitants of paradise. It is the inhabitants of paradise who are the successful ones. (20)
```

```arabic
لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُۥ خَـٰشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ وَتِلْكَ ٱلْأَمْثَـٰلُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] go to pieces with the fear of Allah. We draw such comparisons for mankind, so that they may reflect. (21)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۖ عَـٰلِمُ ٱلْغَيْبِ وَٱلشَّهَـٰدَةِ ۖ هُوَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٢٢)
```

```english
He is Allah—there is no god except Him—Knower of the sensible and the Unseen, He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. (22)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱلْقُدُّوسُ ٱلسَّلَـٰمُ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُ ٱلْمُهَيْمِنُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْجَبَّارُ ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرُ ۚ سُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٢٣)
```

```english
He is Allah—there is no god except Him—the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-benign, the Securer, the All-conserver, the All-mighty, the All-compeller, and the All-magnanimous. Clear is Allah of any partners that they may ascribe [to Him]! (23)
```

```arabic
هُوَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْخَـٰلِقُ ٱلْبَارِئُ ٱلْمُصَوِّرُ ۖ لَهُ ٱلْأَسْمَآءُ ٱلْحُسْنَىٰ ۚ يُسَبِّحُ لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٢٤)
```

```english
He is Allah, the Creator, the Maker, and the Former. To Him belong the Best Names. Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (24)
```

## (Commentary)

The content of these noble verses serves as a conclusion drawn from the preceding verses of the Surah. Reference was made therein to the opposition (مشاقة) of the Banu al-Nadir among the Jews and their breaking of the covenant—which cast them into loss in this world and the Hereafter—as well as the incitement by the hypocrites for them to oppose Allah and His Messenger, which led to their destruction. The true cause behind this was that they did not remain mindful of Allah in their actions and forgot Him; consequently, He caused them to forget their own souls. Thus, they did not choose what contained the good of their souls or their welfare in the immediate or the distant future, and so they wandered aimlessly and perished.

Therefore, it is incumbent upon whoever believes in Allah, His Messenger, and the Last Day to remember his Lord and not forget Him, and to look into what deeds he puts forward for the Day of return to his Lord. Indeed, what he has performed is preserved against him; Allah will call him to account for it on that Day and recompense him with a necessary recompense that shall never leave him.

This is what is intended by His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ** "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow" (59:18). These verses exhort the believers to remember Allah, the Glorified, and not forget Him, and to examine their deeds—upon the righteousness or corruption of which the millstone of their afterlife turns. They should maintain a continuous vigilance (مراقبة) over their actions to ensure they are righteous and sincere for His Noble Face, then call their souls to account (محاسبة), thanking Allah for the good they have done, and rebuking and restraining the soul for the evils it has perpetrated, while seeking forgiveness.

Remembering Allah (ذكر الله) in a manner befitting the courtyard of His Greatness and Majesty—through His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and Sublime Attributes (الصفات العليا) which the Noble Quran has elucidated in its teaching—is the only path that leads its wayfarer to the perfection of servitude (كمال العبودية), and there is no perfection for man above it.

This is because man is a pure servant (عبد محض) and an absolute possession (مملوك طلق) of Allah, the Glorified. He is owned from every conceivable aspect and possesses no independence (استقلال) from any side, just as He, the Exalted, is his Owner from every conceivable aspect and possesses independence from every side. The perfection of a thing lies in its purity in its essence and its effects. Thus, the perfection of man lies in seeing himself as owned by Allah without any independence, and in being characterized by the attributes of servitude—such as submission (خضوع), humility (خشوع), lowliness (ذلة), submissiveness (استكانة), and poverty (فقر)—in relation to the courtyard of Greatness, Might, and Self-Sufficiency (الغنى). Furthermore, his actions and deeds should proceed according to what Allah wills, not what his own soul desires, without any heedlessness in any of these stages: essence (الذات), attributes (الصفات), and actions (الأفعال).

His viewing of his own essence, attributes, and actions with a gaze of pure dependency and absolute ownership cannot be realized except through an inward turning (التوجه الباطني) toward his Lord, Who is a Witness (شهيد) over all things and encompasses (محيط) all things, and Who is the Maintainer (القائم) of every soul as to what it has earned, without ever being heedless of Him or forgetting Him.

At that moment, his heart finds tranquility, as He, the Exalted, said: **أَلَا بِذِكْرِ اللَّهِ تَطْمَئِنُّ الْقُلُوبُ** "Look! The hearts find rest in Allah’s remembrance!" (13:28). He comes to know Allah, the Glorified, through His attributes of perfection contained within His Beautiful Names, and in response to that, the attributes of his own servitude and aspects of deficiency manifest from him—such as submission, humility, lowliness, poverty, and need.

This is followed by his righteous deeds through the permanence of presence (دوام الحضور) and the continuity of remembrance (استمرار الذكر). The Exalted said: **وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ فِي نَفْسِكَ تَضَرُّعًا وَخِيفَةً وَدُونَ الْجَهْرِ مِنَ الْقَوْلِ بِالْغُدُوِّ وَالْآصَالِ وَلَا تَكُن مِّنَ الْغَافِلِينَ إِنَّ الَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِ وَيُسَبِّحُونَهُ وَلَهُ يَسْجُدُونَ** "And remember your Lord within your self submissively and fearfully, and without raising your voice, at morning and evening, and do not be among the heedless. Indeed those who are [stationed] near your Lord do not disdain His worship, and they glorify Him and prostrate to Him" (7:205-206). And He said: **فَإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِندَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهَارِ وَهُمْ لَا يَسْأَمُونَ** "But if they are disdainful, those who are near your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire" (41:38).

To what we have mentioned regarding knowing Him, the Exalted, through His attributes of perfection and knowing the soul through its corresponding attributes of deficiency and need, His saying points by the requirement of the context: **لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ** "Had We sent down this Quran..." (59:21) until the end of the verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ** "O you who have faith! Be wary of Allah, and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow" (59:18) until the end of the verse. This is a command to the believers to have godwariness (تقوى) — *the protective shield of the soul through obedience to Allah* — and another command, which is to look into the deeds they have sent forward for the Day of Reckoning: are they righteous, such that they may hope for Allah's reward through them, or are they corrupt, such that they should fear Allah's punishment for them and rectify them through repentance (التوبة) and turning back (الإنابة)? This is the accounting of the soul (محاسبة النفس).

As for godwariness (التقوى), it has been explained in the traditions as "piety regarding the prohibitions of Allah" (الورع عن محارم الله). Since it relates to both obligations and prohibitions together, it constitutes the avoidance of neglecting obligations and the avoidance of committing prohibitions.

As for "considering what the soul has sent ahead for tomorrow," it is another matter beyond godwariness. Its relation to godwariness is like the relation of a secondary corrective gaze by a worker toward his work or a craftsman toward what he has fashioned, in order to perfect it and rectify the deficiencies he was heedless of or the mistakes he made during the process of work and production.

Thus, it is incumbent upon all believers to be wary of Allah regarding the duties directed toward them, obeying Him and not disobeying Him, and then to look into the deeds they have sent forward—by which they shall live tomorrow after being called to account for them—whether they are righteous, so their reward is hoped for, or corrupt, so their punishment is feared, and thus they repent to Allah and seek His forgiveness.

This is a general duty that encompasses every believer because of the universal need to rectify one's deeds, and the consideration of some does not suffice for the consideration of others. However, those among the people of faith who actually perform this are extremely few, to the point of being almost non-existent. To this, the phrasing of the verse alludes: **وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ** "and let a soul consider."

So His saying: **وَلْتَنظُرْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ** "and let every soul consider what it sends ahead for tomorrow" is a general address to all believers. However, since those occupied with this consideration among the people of faith—indeed, even among the people of godwariness—are in the utmost minority, nearly non-existent due to their preoccupation with the fleeting interests of the world and the absorption of their time in managing livelihoods and rectifying the affairs of life, the address was delivered in the third person (الغيبة) and attached to an indefinite soul (نفس ما), saying: "and let a soul consider." In this type of address, despite the duty being general by nature, there is a rebuke and a reprimand for the believers, along with an allusion to the scarcity of those among them who are fit to comply with it.

His saying: **مَا قَدَّمَتْ لِغَدٍ** "what it sends ahead for tomorrow" is an inquiry into the nature of the deed sent ahead for tomorrow and an elucidation of the "consideration." It is also possible that **مَا** is a relative pronoun, and it, along with its relative clause, is the object of the "consideration."

What is meant by "tomorrow" (غد) is the Day of Resurrection, which is the day of the reckoning of deeds. It was expressed as "tomorrow" to indicate its proximity to them, like the proximity of tomorrow to its yesterday. The Exalted said: **إِنَّهُمْ يَرَوْنَهُ بَعِيدًا وَنَرَاهُ قَرِيبًا** "Indeed they see it to be far off, and We see it to be near" (70:6-7).

The meaning is: O you who have faith, be wary of Allah by obeying Him in all that He commands you and forbids you, and let a soul among you look into what it has performed of deeds, and let it see what it has sent forward of its actions for the Day of Reckoning—is it a righteous deed or a corrupt one? And is its righteous deed truly righteous and accepted, or is it rejected?

His saying: **وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ** "and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is well aware of what you do" (59:18) is a second command for godwariness, and **إِنَّ اللَّهَ خَبِيرٌ** "Indeed Allah is well aware..." is its justification. Justifying this godwariness by His being aware of the deeds indicates that the godwariness commanded a second time is godwariness in the station of accounting and considering deeds in terms of their rectification, their sincerity for Allah, the Glorified, and their preservation from what corrupts them. As for His saying at the beginning of the verse: **اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ** "Be wary of Allah," what is meant is godwariness in the very performance of deeds by restricting them to acts of obedience and avoiding acts of disobedience.

From here it becomes clear that the meaning of godwariness in the two instances is different: the first is godwariness in the original performance of deeds, and the second is godwariness regarding the performed deeds in terms of their rectification and sincerity.

It also becomes apparent that the statement of some—that the first is for repentance from past sins and the second is for avoiding future disobedience—is not sound. Similar is what has been said: that the first is for performing obligations and the second is for abandoning prohibitions; or that the second command is merely for the emphasis of the first.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ فَأَنسَاهُمْ أَنفُسَهُمْ** "And do not be like those who forgot Allah, so He made them forget their own souls" (59:19) etc. **Forgetfulness** (النسيان) is the disappearance of the form of the known from the soul after it had been present therein, along with the disappearance of its origin. It is used broadly to refer to any turning away (الإعراض) from a thing by not according it any practical effect. The Exalted said: **وَقِيلَ الْيَوْمَ نَنسَاكُمْ كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ لِقَاءَ يَوْمِكُمْ هَٰذَا وَمَأْوَاكُمُ النَّارُ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن نَّاصِرِينَ** "It will be said, ‘Today We will forget you just as you forgot the encounter of this day of yours. Your abode will be the Fire, and you will not have any helpers’" (45:34).

The verse, according to its core meaning, is like an emphasis on the content of the previous verse, as if it were said: Send forward for the Day of Reckoning and Recompense a righteous deed by which your souls shall live, and do not forget it. Furthermore, since the cause of forgetting the soul is forgetting Allah—for by forgetting Him, the Exalted, His Beautiful Names and Sublime Attributes are forgotten, to which the essential attributes of man (such as lowliness, poverty, and need) are linked—man then imagines himself to be independent in existence. He fancies that he possesses for himself life, power, knowledge, and the rest of the perfections that appear to him, and he views the other apparent cosmic causes as his peers in independence, affecting him and being affected by him.

At that point, he relies upon himself, whereas he ought to have relied upon his Lord; he hopes in and fears the apparent causes, whereas he ought to have hoped in and feared his Lord; he finds tranquility in other than his Lord, whereas he ought to have found tranquility in his Lord.

In short, he forgets his Lord and the return to Him, and he turns away from Him by turning toward others. From this follows that he forgets his soul; for what he fancies of his soul—that it is an independently existing being, owning the perfections of existence that appear in it, and that the management of its affairs belongs to it, drawing support from the cosmic causes around it—is not the human being. Rather, the human being is a creature whose existence is dependent (متعلق الوجود), who is total ignorance, total weakness, total lowliness, total poverty, and so on. Whatever perfection he possesses—such as existence, knowledge, power, honor, and self-sufficiency—belongs to his Lord and terminates in his Lord, and the other cosmic causes are like him in that regard.

In conclusion, since the cause of forgetting the soul is forgetting Allah, the prohibition of forgetting the soul in the verse was shifted to a prohibition of forgetting Him, the Exalted, because the cessation of the effect through the cessation of its cause is more eloquent and emphatic. He did not settle for a mere general prohibition of forgetting Him—by saying, "and do not forget Allah, lest He make you forget yourselves"—but rather proceeded by giving the ruling through an example to be more effective and closer to acceptance. Thus, He forbade them from being like those who forgot Allah, referring to those mentioned previously among the Jews of Banu al-Nadir and Banu Qaynuqa' and those whose state is like theirs in opposing Allah and His Messenger.

So He said: **وَلَا تَكُونُوا كَالَّذِينَ نَسُوا اللَّهَ** "And do not be like those who forgot Allah," then He branched from it His saying: **فأنساهُمْ أنفُسَهُمْ** "so He made them forget their own souls," as the branching of an effect from its cause. Then He followed it with His saying: **أُولَٰئِكَ هُمُ الْفَاسِقُونَ** "it is they who are the transgressors," indicating that they are truly transgressors (فاسقون), having exited the garb of servitude.

The verse, although it forbids forgetting Him—which results in forgetting the soul—nevertheless, by its occurrence in the context of the previous verse, commands the remembrance of Allah and vigilance (مراقبة) over Him.

It has become clear from all that preceded in the two verses that the first verse commands the accounting of the soul (محاسبة النفس) and the second commands remembrance (الذكر) and vigilance (المراقبة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَا يَسْتَوِي أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ وَأَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ أَصْحَابُ الْجَنَّةِ هُمُ الْفَائِزُونَ** "The inhabitants of the Fire and the inhabitants of paradise are not equal. It is the inhabitants of paradise who are the winners" (59:20). Al-Raghib said: **Success** (الفوز) is the attainment of good along with the achievement of safety. The context bears witness that what is meant by "the inhabitants of the Fire" are those who forget Allah, and by "the inhabitants of Paradise" are those who remember Allah and are vigilant.

The verse is a complete proof for the necessity of joining those who remember Allah and are vigilant over Him, rather than those who forget. Its formulation is that there are two groups and no third: those who remember Allah and those who forget Him. Man must necessarily join one of the two. They are not equal such that the two joinings would be equal and man would not care which he joined. Rather, there is a preferred and a non-preferred, and it is mandatory to choose the preferred over the non-preferred. Preference belongs to the group of those who remember, for they are the successful ones and none else. Thus, the preference is for their side, so it is mandatory for every soul to choose to join the group of those who remember.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَّرَأَيْتَهُ خَاشِعًا مُّتَصَدِّعًا مِّنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ** "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] rent asunder out of the fear of Allah" (59:21) etc. In *al-Majma'*: **Splitting** (التصدع) is the separation after cohesion, similar to "cleaving asunder" (التفطر).

The speech is presented as a parable (مثل) based on imagery (تخييل), and the proof for this is His saying at the end of the verse: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ** "We draw such parables for mankind..." (59:21).

The intent is to magnify the matter of the Quran by virtue of the realities of knowledge, the foundations of laws, the lessons, the exhortations, the promises, and the threats it contains—it being the speech of Allah, the Great. The meaning is: If the mountain were something upon which it were permissible to send down the Quran, and We sent it down upon it, you would have seen it—despite its hardness, its harshness, its massive size, and its strength of resistance against calamities—affected and rent asunder out of the fear of Allah. If this is the state of the mountain as it is, then man is more worthy of being humbled before Allah when he recites it or it is recited to him. How strange is the state of the people of opposition and stubbornness; their hearts do not soften for it, nor do they humble themselves or fear.

The shift (التفات) from the first-person plural ("We") to the third person in His saying: **مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ** "out of the fear of Allah" is to indicate the cause of the ruling; for the mountain only humbles itself and rends asunder at the descent of the Quran because it is the speech of Allah, exalted is His Name.

His saying: **وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ** "We draw such parables for mankind so that they may reflect" (59:21) is the placement of a universal ruling in the place of a particular one to indicate that the ruling is not unique to its instance but is applicable and pervasive in many other instances.

So His saying: **لَوْ أَنزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ** "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain..." is a parable Allah has drawn for mankind regarding the matter of the Quran to bring closer its greatness and the majesty of its status—inasmuch as it is the speech of Allah and because of the knowledge it contains—in the hope that people will reflect upon it. Thus, they would receive the Quran with the reception it deserves, realize the manifest truth within it, and be guided to what it leads to of the path of servitude (طريق العبودية), beyond which there is no path to their perfection and happiness. Included in this is what was mentioned in the previous verses regarding vigilance (المراقبة) and accounting (المحاسبة).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ هُوَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ الرَّحِيمُ** "He is Allah—there is no god except Him—Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible, He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful" (59:22). This verse and the two verses after it, although presented to enumerate a group of His Beautiful Names and to point to His being named by every most beautiful name and His being glorified by the testimony of what is in the heavens and the earth, nevertheless, when joined with the preceding command for remembrance, they imply that those who remember should remember Him by His Beautiful Names and thus know their own souls through the corresponding names of deficiency. Understand this.

And by joining them with the previous verse and what it contains of His saying: **مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ** "out of the fear of Allah," they serve to justify the mountain's humbling and rending asunder out of the fear of Allah. It is as if it were said: And how could it not be so, when He is Allah, besides Whom there is no god, the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible... until the end of the verses.

His saying: **هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "He is Allah—there is no god except Him" provides, through the relative pronoun and its clause, the meaning of one of His names, which is His Oneness (وحدانيته) in His divinity and His being the only object of worship. Some of what relates to the declaration of oneness (التهليل) has already preceded in the commentary on His saying: **وَإِلَٰهُكُمْ إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "Your God is the one God; there is no god except Him" (2:163).

His saying: **عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ** "Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible." **The Sensible** (الشهادة) is that which is witnessed and present before the perceiver, and **the Unseen** (الغيب) is its opposite. These are two relative meanings; it is possible for a thing to be "sensible" in relation to one thing and "unseen" in relation to another. The matter revolves around a type of encompassment (إحاطة) of the thing—whether through sense, imagination, intellect, or existence—which is "the sensible," and its absence, which is "the unseen." Every assumed unseen or sensible thing is, as such, encompassed by Him and known. Thus, He is the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible, while others have no knowledge of the unseen due to the limitation of their existence and their lack of encompassment, except for what He has taught, as He said: **عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِن رَّسُولٍ** "Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of" (72:26-27). As for Him, the Exalted, He is the Unseen in an absolute sense; there is no way for anything to encompass Him at all, as He said: **وَلَا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْمًا** "but they do not comprehend Him in [their] knowledge" (20:110).

His saying: **هُوَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ الرَّحِيمُ** "He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful." The discussion on the meaning of these two names has already preceded in the commentary on Surah al-Fatiha.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْمَلِكُ الْقُدُّوسُ السَّلَامُ الْمُؤْمِنُ الْمُهَيْمِنُ الْعَزِيزُ الْجَبَّارُ الْمُتَكَبِّرُ** "He is Allah—there is no god except Him—the Sovereign, the All-holy, the All-benign, the Securer, the All-preserver, the All-mighty, the All-compeller, the All-magnanimous" (59:23) etc. **The Sovereign** (الملك) is the Owner of the management of people's affairs and the ruling over them. **The All-holy** (القدوس) is an intensive form of holiness (القدس), which is sanctity and purity. **The All-benign** (السلام) is He who meets you with peace and well-being without evil or harm. **The Securer** (المؤمن) is He who grants security. **The All-preserver** (المهيمن) is the Superior who dominates over a thing.

**The All-mighty** (العزيز) is the Victor whom nothing overcomes, or He who possesses what others possess without the reverse. **The All-compeller** (الجبار) is an intensive form from "mending a fracture" (جبر الكسر), or He whose will is executed and who compels as He pleases. **The All-magnanimous** (المتكبر) is He who is clothed in greatness (الكبرياء) and manifests through it.

His saying: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Clear is Allah of any partners they may ascribe to Him!" (59:23) is a praise of Him, the Exalted, as in His saying: **وَقَالُوا اتَّخَذَ اللَّهُ وَلَدًا سُبْحَانَهُ** "And they say, ‘Allah has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!" (2:116).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ اللَّهُ الْخَالِقُ الْبَارِئُ الْمُصَوِّرُ** "He is Allah, the Creator, the Maker, the Shaper" (59:24) until the end of the verse. **The Creator** (الخالق) is the Bringer of things into existence according to a measure (تقدير). **The Maker** (البارئ) is the Originator of things, making them distinct from one another. **The Shaper** (المصور) is the Giver of forms to them, by which they are distinguished from one another. These three names contain the meaning of bringing into existence (الإيجاد) from different considerations, and there is an order between them: shaping is a branch of making, and making is a branch of creating, as is evident.

The two previous verses began with His saying: **الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ** "besides Whom there is no god," describing "Allah" with it and following it with the names, unlike this verse where He said: **هُوَ اللَّهُ الْخَالِقُ** "He is Allah, the Creator..."

This is because the noble names mentioned in the two previous verses—which are eleven names—are among the requirements of Lordship (الربوبية) and the ownership of management (مالكية التدبير), from which divinity (الألوهية) and being the rightful object of worship (المعبودية بالحق) branch out. These belong essentially and independently to Allah, the Glorified, alone, without any partner for Him in that. Thus, His being characterized alone by them necessitates the exclusivity of divinity and the entitlement to worship for Him, the Exalted.

Therefore, the noble names are in the position of a justification for the exclusivity of divinity to Him, the Exalted, as if it were said: There is no god but He because He is the Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible, He is the All-beneficent, the All-merciful. For this reason also, He concluded these names with His saying as a praise of Him: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Clear is Allah of any partners they may ascribe to Him!" in refutation of the claim of partners as the polytheists say.

As for His saying: **هُوَ اللَّهُ الْخَالِقُ الْبَارِئُ الْمُصَوِّرُ**, the names mentioned therein provide the meaning of creation and bringing into existence. The exclusivity of that to Him, the Exalted, does not [necessarily] necessitate the exclusivity of divinity to Him [in the view of some], as indicated by the fact that the idolaters believe in the exclusivity of creation and bringing into existence to Him, the Exalted, yet they nevertheless call upon lords and gods besides Him and affirm partners for Him.

As for the occurrence of the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) at the beginning of all three verses, it is the proper name for the Essence (الذات) that gathers all attributes of perfection, to which all names are linked and upon which they proceed. In the repetition, there is added emphasis and consolidation of the objective.

His saying: **لَهُ الْأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَى** "To Him belong the Best Names" (59:24) is an allusion to the rest of the Beautiful Names in their entirety, because "the names" (الأسماء) is a plural defined by the article "al-", which signifies generality.

His saying: **يُسَبِّحُ لَهُ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** "Whatever there is in the heavens and the earth glorifies Him" (59:24), meaning all the creatures in the world, even the heavens and the earth themselves. The explanation of the meaning of this sentence has already preceded repeatedly.

Then He concluded the verses with His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** "and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise" (59:24), meaning the Victor who is never overcome, whose action is precise (متقن) and contains no randomness. Thus, the disobedience of the sinners and the opposition of the stubborn do not frustrate Him in what He has legislated and called toward, nor does the obedience of the obedient or the reward of the virtuous go to waste with Him.

The concern for concluding the speech with these two names—and the allusion thereby to the fact that the Quran sent down from Him is an "all-mighty" and "all-wise" speech—is what called for the repetition of His name "the All-mighty" and its mention with "the All-wise," despite its previous mention among the names.

The Quran has also been described with might and wisdom, as He said: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتَابٌ عَزِيزٌ** "and indeed it is an august Book" (41:41), and He said: **وَالْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ** "By the wise Quran" (36:2).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Majma'*: Regarding His saying, the Exalted: **عَالِمُ الْغَيْبِ وَالشَّهَادَةِ** "Knower of the Unseen and the Sensible," narrated from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), he said: "The Unseen (الغيب) is what has not yet been, and the Sensible (الشهادة) is what has already been."

**I say:** This is an interpretation through some of the instances. We have already cited traditions from them (peace be upon them) regarding the meaning of the Majestic Name (اسم الجلالة) and the two names "the All-beneficent" (الرحمن) and "the All-merciful" (الرحيم) under the commentary on the *Basmalah* in Surah al-Fatiha.

In *al-Tawhid*, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) in a tradition: **لم يزل حيا بلا حياة وملكا قادرا ـ قبل أن ينشئ شيئا وملكا جبارا ـ بعد إنشائه للكون** — "He has never ceased to be Living without a life, and a Powerful King—before He originated anything—and a Compelling King—after His origination of the universe."

**I say:** His saying: "He has never ceased to be Living without a life" means without a life added to the Essence. His saying: "He has never ceased to be a Powerful King before He originated anything" is a referral of "King" (الملك)—which is among the attributes of action—to "Power" (القدرة)—which is among the attributes of the Essence—so that its realization before bringing into existence may be sound.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Hisham al-Jawaliqi, he said: I asked Abu 'Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah: **سُبْحَانَ اللَّهِ** (Glory be to Allah), what does He mean by it? He said: "Glorification (تنزيه) — *the declaration of His absolute transcendence above all deficiency and likeness*."

In *Nahj al-Balagha*: **والخالق لا بمعنى حركة ونصب** — "And the Creator, not in the sense of movement or toil."

**I say:** We have already cited a number of narrations regarding the Beautiful Names and their enumeration in the discussion on the Beautiful Names in the eighth volume of this book.

In the famous Prophetic narration: **حاسبوا أنفسكم قبل أن تحاسبوا ـ وزنوا قبل أن توزنوا وتجهزوا للغرض الأكبر** — "Call your souls to account before you are called to account, and weigh [your deeds] before you are weighed, and prepare yourselves for the greatest presentation."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abu al-Hasan al-Madi (peace be upon him), he said: **ليس منا من لم يحاسب نفسه في كل يوم فإن عمل حسنا ازداد لله شكرا وإن عمل سيئا استغفر الله وتاب إليه** — "He is not of us who does not call his soul to account every day; if he has performed a good deed, he increases in gratitude to Allah, and if he has performed an evil deed, he seeks Allah's forgiveness and repents to Him."

**I say:** There are other narrations with a meaning close to this. We have also cited narrations from them (peace be upon them) regarding the meaning of the remembrance of Allah (ذكر الله) under the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **فَاذْكُرُونِي أَذْكُرْكُمْ** "Remember Me, and I will remember you" (2:152), and His saying: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا اذْكُرُوا اللَّهَ ذِكْرًا كَثِيرًا** "O you who have faith! Remember Allah with frequent remembrance" (33:41). Let whoever wishes refer to them.
